---
title: "Reference Systems - Map Projections part 2"
author: "Poshan Niraula"
format: 
  revealjs:
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    logo: 2021-logo-ifgi-text-de.png
    incremental: false
    css: logo.css
    width: 1600
    height: 900
editor: visual
---

## Azimuthal Projections

### General Azimuthal 

- Formed by bringing a plane into contact with sphere or ellipsoid
- Scale factor distortion will be circularly symmetric
- Special case is Polar projection, where plane touches Poles
- Overall scaling possible such that the scale factor at the center is less than 1

## Azimuthal Projections

### Azimuthal equidistant

- Kept scale factor = 1, in the direction radial from the center of the projection.
- Polar equidistant - scale factor along meridian is $k_m$ = 1
- Distances from center will be correct

## Azimuthal Projections

### Azimuthal equal area

- Formed similar to Azimuthal equidistant but changing the scale factors such that:
  - scale factor of lines radial along the centre are inverse of scale fator in the perpendicular direction

- Example: Lambert Equal area 2001 used by European statistical mapping

## Azimuthal Projections

### Stereographic/conformal

- Conformal version of Azimuthal
- Graphical can be constructed by setting viewing Point on the opposite side of the Earth from the center of projection
- Polar stereographic used in complement the UTM in beyond latitude 84 degrees N and 80 degrees South = UPS
- UPS: scale factor of pole = 0.994 -> overall scaling

- ![](images/figure327.png)


## Azimuthal Projections

### Stereographic/conformal - oblique

- Oblique aspect is tangential to chosen point(origin)
- Or if oveall scaling applied scale factor is true at the small circle centred at the origin
- Used in ellipsoidal forms in places where the range of latitude is similar to range of longitude
- Eg. Netherlands RD, Romania Stereo 70, Prince Edward Island Stereographic


<!-- ## Azimuthal Projections

### Stereographic/conformal- Double Stereographic

- In ellipsoidal case, steps taken are :

1. Ellipsoidal coordinates are transformed to spherical

2. Project the spherical coordinates onto the grids

- On step 1, two approaces are possible: 

1. Convert the ellipsoidal coordinates to a conformal sphere with a radius based on the ellipsoidal radii at the projection origin or  

2. use radius based on the ellipsoidal radii at each point

This second method is sometimes also called "Double stereographic" -->


<!-- ## Azimuthal Projections

### Gnomonic

- formed by projecting all the points from center of earth
- seldomly used
- scale factor distortion becomes extreme away from the centre of projeciton
- Only advantage: Great circles (shortest routes between two points) are straight lines and vice versa,  but this task is replaced by computational techniques

 -->

## Azimuthal Projections
### Discussion questions:
- Why does the UPS projection use a standard parallel at 81 degrees?


## Azimuthal Projections

### Azimuthal orthographic

- formed by projecting all points on teh ellipsoid onto a plane, along lines that are normal to the plane.
- Viewing height is infinity
- Resulting in view of Earth analogous to how it would look from deep space using a telescope

![](images/figure332.png)

## Azimuthal Projections

### Azimuthal perspective projection

- Similar to the Azimuthal orthographic but with finite view point
- used by Google Earth- not purely perspective as it accounts for height of the terrain
- ![](images/figure334.png)


## Conic

### General Conic

- Formed by bringing a cone into contact with the sphere/ellipsoid
- Touches the sphere/ellipsoid along a parallel of latitude -\> standard parallel
- different shapes of cones can be selected
- ![](images/figure335.png)

## Conic

- equivalent of overall scaling achieved by using **two standard parallels**
- applicable when the region of interest is broad extent in longitude and regions that are in mid-latitude

## Conic

### Conic equidistant

- preserves scale factor along the meridian ($k_m$ = 1)


## Conic

### Albers equal area/ Conic equal area projection

- ![](images/figure338.png)
- Pole becomes a circular arc -> shape has been compromised to keep area undistorted
- Compare wiht the cylindrical equal area the distortion is less because of mid-latitude and large east-west extent of Europe.


## Conic

### Lambert Conformal Conic (LCC)

-  widely used projection
- ![](images/figure339.png)
- Meridian meet at pole
- if standard parallel set at equator the meridians meet at infinity (they are parallel) same as Mercator
- and if standard parallel set at 90 degrees would be same as polar stereographic
- not to be used at extreme cases

## Conic

### Lambert Conformal Conic (LCC)

- Zoned LCC: example France Lambert 
- Zones are tiered North to South
- Zone boundaries are set midway between the parallels of origin for adjacency zones
- Other examples of LCC are Europe conformal 2001 (std parallel 1 at 65$^\circ$ N and 2 at 35 $^\circ$ N), Tennessee state plane CS83, Geoscience Australia Lambert 

## Conic

### Oblique conic 
- ![](images/figure342.png)
- Example of conformal case is Krovak projection used in Czech Republic and Slovakia

## Web Mercator

- Major problem: 
  - ellipsoidal model of earth + **Mercator projection formula for sphere** 

- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=R-ENwz5u7pE

## Discussion question
**"When designing a web-based geographical application using libraries like Leaflet, are there libraries used to dynamically reproject vector data and basemaps on-the-fly based on the user's area of interest?"**

  - **Proj4js / proj4-leaflet** — https://kartena.github.io/Proj4Leaflet/
  - **OpenLayers** — native multi-projection support (unlike Leaflet, which is locked to Web Mercator by default)
  - **MapLibre GL JS** — supports globe view and alternative projections (recent)


## Adaptive composite projection
https://berniejenny.info/demos/AdaptiveCompositeMapProjections/

## Choice of Projection system
https://www.explainxkcd.com/wiki/index.php/977:_Map_Projections


## Section 3.6 to 3.9

Reading guides:

1.  What parameters are required to define different classes of projection?
2.  What is role of scale factor in computing within map projections?
3.  What factors should be considered while designing a map projection?

## Non-geometric projection method
- with advanced technologies, it is possible to create the projections in which the central line follows feature of interest, not just meridians or parallels
- Formula $(E, N) = f(\phi, \lambda)$, here $f$ gets more complicated 
- Example: NewZealand (to fit the shape of country), Space oblique Mercator (to follow the satellite ground track), Snake projection

## Snake Projection
 - for linear features a standard projeciton like TM, LCC has a scale factor vaying along the route
 - creates map distances not equal to ground distance and error gets higher further away from the central meridian/standard parallel
 - Traditionally fixed by applying ellipsoidal corrections and scale factor correction
 - But when using modern systems like automated track guidance
 - Snake Projections when incorporated in such systems makes scale is almost true along the track and ground distance = grid distance so the automated machines work error free.


## Computing wihtin map projections
- Main types of computations done are:
  - calculate distances, area, bearing
  - Transfer the survey measurements of distances and angles to projections

- distance on the projection = distance on the ellipsoid * $k$
- scale factor is the key here and should be closely associated with the tolerable degree of error

## Discussion question


- **Q1:** *"What is the significance of the scale factor varying across a map projection?"*

- **Q2:** *"Are there typical thresholds (e.g. maximum scale error in ppm) beyond which you would recommend switching to a different projection or redefining parameters?"*


## Computing wihtin map projections - whole earth case

- Usually the computations on the map projections that are designed for the whole earth should be avoided
- As k can be very large (sometimes even infinity)
- Some exception examples:
  - Distances computed from central point on Azimuthal equidistant projection are undistorted.
  - Area on the equal area projection are correct, but distances are distorted.

## Computing wihtin map projections - Examples
### Topographic mapping

- Area that the maps cover have been restricted such that the distortion of scale factor away from unity is within reasonable limits: usually within 0.1%
- And for many applications this is a acceptable limit. Example: distance of an Emergency response vehicle from the scene of accident, computing population that lives within given radius of a supermarket. 

## Computing wihtin map projections - Examples
### Area computation

- Computation of area of a farmer's field, for land valuation or crop yield estimation.
- Here scale factor should be applied in two dimensions
- area on the ellipsoid = area on projection / $k^2$, 
- assuming k is same in both dimensions (conformality), in case of equal area projection the scale factor is reciprocal on direction to another

## Discussion question

**"The book states that it's unusual to see map projection parameter values printed on maps. Why might this be the case? If someone already went to all the effort to create the map, it should be easy enough to include the values of the parameters..."**

- Hints:
  - Who is the **target audience** of a printed map?
  - Is the parameter info **truly missing**, or just **somewhere else**?
  - Commercial / proprietary reasons?
  - How have **digital metadata standards** (EPSG, ISO 19115) changed this?

## Discussion question

**"Why are areas computed on an equal-area projection correct, but distances are wrong?"**

- ![*Hint:*](images/figure36.png) 


## Computing wihtin map projections - Examples
### Land Surveying

- Accuracy Requirement is very high 
- The elevation above the ellipsoid is also considered here.

- Theoretically $k$ changes in each point, but in practice it changes slowly across a projection that a single scale factor can be considered applicable to all distances in one survey
- Rate of change of $k$: depends on particular projection
- Most extreme rate of change is on the edge of the projection, over 5 km it vary from 1.00043 to 1.00039 amounting error 8cm if not averaged, 3cm in 3km.
- To achieve accuracy of 1cm over 1 km scale factor for each line is computed: by mean of end point values of scale factor or by Simpson's rule 


## Computing wihtin map projections - Examples
### Land Surveying

- **Corrections to angles** is also required as the straight lines observed between two points does not plot straight line on projection
- The magnitude of this correction is never large: eg. at the extermes of TM zone of width 3 degrees: 3s over 5km and 6s over 10km. 
- **Property of convergence**: angle between grid north and true north, can reach 2-3 degrees even in the survey projections

## Discussion question

- **"What is Unitisation in map projection computations?"**
- What are the factors that you would consider in designing the map projections?
- **"Why can't there be one map projection that preserves all these properties (area, shape, distance) accurately at the same time?"**

## Discussion question
-*"When should we choose projections like Mercator over Equal Area projections in real-world applications? I am confused about how people decide which projection is best."*

- Match the use case to the projection:
  - Marine navigation → ?
  - Crop area statistics → ?
  - Long-distance flight planning → ?
  - National topographic map → ?

List of Projections:
  - Mercator (Conformal Cylindrical)
  - Albers Equal-Area Conic
  - Gnomonic
  - Transverse Mercator / Lambert Conformal Conic
  - Polar stereographic
  - Robinson

## Next Lecture
Date: 11.06.2026

- Reading: Chapter 4.1 - 4.4 of the coursebook
- Reading guides:

1. What are two key differences between a conversion and a
transformation?
2. What is the rst step common to any transformation
endeavor?
3. What does a grid interpolation do (and why is it needed)?
4. What are similarities and differences between an Helmert
transformation and a Molodensky transformation?
